← Works Case study · Auto Trader UK
AT Auto Trader · Digital Retailing 2020–2021

We already owned the search. The question was whether to own the trust.

By spring 2020 every forecourt in the country had gone dark overnight, and the industry's instinct was retreat. The data said the opposite. Demand hadn't disappeared — it had moved, fast, into a journey nobody had finished building. The deeper we looked, the clearer the blocker became. It was never the buy button. It was confidence.

My role
Lead Experience Designer — discovery, strategy, design direction
The brief
Turn standalone retail products into one service-led journey
Context
COVID lockdowns; an online-only disruptor scaling fast
The reframe
Not a transaction problem. A trust problem.
The decision we had to de-risk

Could Auto Trader bring part-exchange, finance, reservations and options into the search experience — without breaking the trust the marketplace had earned over 40 years?

A Why now — the stakes
01 · The advantage we held A captive audience, mid-pandemic The hardest, most expensive thing in retail — attention — the marketplace already had at record scale. We weren't building a destination. We were finishing a journey millions were already on.
15.5m
Cross-platform visits in a single Lockdown-3 week — up 24% on 2019.
Auto Trader · 2021
+60%
Growth in leads to retailers vs 2019 — contact made before any forecourt visit.
Auto Trader · 2020
61→72%
Buyers who found digital car-buying appealing, year on year — across 5,000 people.
Auto Trader · 2020→21
8.75/10
Consumer confidence in affording their next car — highest since tracking began.
Auto Trader · survey
02 · The pressure & the prize A disruptor was already taking the deal

While we deliberated, someone was capturing the high-margin layers

An online-only player was proving, in public and at speed, that people would complete the whole journey online — and capture the finance and ancillary revenue our classified model never touched. "Wait and see" had a price tag that rose every quarter. The prize: Europe's $700bn used-car market was under 2% online.

OCT 2020£2bnCazoo valuation doubled in a £240m raise — revenues already ~£100m, ten months after launch
MAR 2021$7bnNYSE listing announced
H1 2021+521%YoY revenue to £248m; 20,454 vehicles, up 401%
→ Where the market data ran out
Every survey agreed appetite was real — and that reluctance was too. The job was never "build buy buttons." It was moving customers to action.

The market told us that people would buy online. It couldn't tell us how to make them. So we went and asked them.

B What buyers actually told us
03 · Deep customer empathy Five projective depth interviews, behind the survey numbers We ran filmed depth interviews with recent and prospective online buyers to surface the subconscious stuff a survey can't — the associations, fears and rituals around a purchase people rank alongside buying a house. Three findings reframed the whole project.
Finding 01"Online" is a spectrum, not a switch
1st degree

Research online

Research onlineDealer visitDealer completion
2nd degree

Complete online, see it first

Research onlineDealer visitCompletion online
3rd degree

Fully online, delivered

Research onlineNo dealer visitCompletion onlineDelivery to home
Direction of the market →

Most buyers don't want fully online. They want to do the anxious admin from the sofa, then keep the right to see the car in the flesh. That insight killed the "buy blind" model before we drew a screen.

Finding 02Two buyers, two appetites for risk
Segment

Confident Connoisseurs

Experienced, love the research, know what they want. Comfortable completing online — sometimes impulsively.

know carsgo with their gutmore confident online
Segment

Edgy Explorers

More nervous, less sure, need reassurance. Treat the dealer as a proxy guarantee and do less of the journey online.

need convincinghand-holdingdealer = safety

One journey had to carry both — confident enough to get out of the Connoisseur's way, reassuring enough to walk the Explorer through.

Finding 03The live experience is online's blind spot

"You see it from one side on the internet. When you go in, you see the reality… I need to get it right."

— Buying new, over £30K

"From experience I completely trust it — as long as you've done your research and know what you want."

— Bought used online, under £15K

The buying process is overwhelmingly visual, and every step is a potential exit back to the forecourt. But the levers that keep people online were universal across every buyer and every sector we looked at:

transparencyhonesty — good & bada named humansimplicityreassurance at every stepclear guaranteeseasy returnsaccountability
C The reframe — trust is the product

Synthesised across the qual and the market data, the buyer's own words pointed at one answer: the ideal online experience blends the best of offline with online — to build trust and confidence. Auto Trader didn't need to become a retailer. It needed to extend what it was famous for, carrying 40 years of earned trust the whole way. We broke "trust" into something we could actually design against.

#1 · Competence
You can do what you say.

The product works — quality checks, accurate valuations, a quote that holds.

#2 · Dependability
You'll do it every time.

One consistent experience across every platform and channel.

#3 · Integrity
You'll be fair.

No pressure, no ambush, honesty about the good and the bad.

Trust framework · Edelman Trust Barometer — a majority now trust brands over institutions
↗ Bring online

The friction-heavy steps buyers were begging to start from the sofa — captured as committed deals, not cold leads, wrapped in assurances that engineer confidence.

⚓ Keep on the forecourt

The inspection, the test drive, the human anchor. The disruptor who designed this out would later prove the point the hard way.

→ The reframe that became the spine
The bottleneck was never demand. It was trust, sitting between a record audience and a deal they were ready to do.

Not online vs forecourt. Not products vs journey. A service-led, trust-anchored blended experience — and everything that followed was built to deliver it.

D The design response — three experiments

Trust had to be made felt at the three moments confidence collapses: valuing the car you're trading in, working out finance, and the leap from sofa to forecourt. We prototyped and tested each — lo-fi first, brand protected, the "Helen" voice carried from the products that already worked.

Stream 01 · Guaranteed Part-ExchangeAnxiety into a guaranteed price

The fear was never the form. It was being low-balled at the gate — so people never started the deal at all.

A valuation only builds confidence if the buyer believes the number and didn't have to fight for it. So GPX became a calm self-appraisal you do from your own driveway, ending in a guaranteed price, held for seven days.

  • Guided condition flow — history, bodywork, wheels, tyres, windows, interior.
  • An assumptions shortcut — we pre-fill the rare stuff; you only correct what's wrong.
  • Help where it's hard — the 20p tyre test, the 5p chip test, drawn not described.

The result screen does the one thing a lowball quote never does — it shows its working: the condition you reported, live market value, the data behind it. Then it hands control back: take it as cash, put it toward a car, or advertise privately.

7 days
Guaranteed price, held — no haggling at the gate
5
Step self-appraisal, from the driveway
3
Ways to use the value
↳ Trust mechanism

A fixed price, full transparency, control. Remove the ambush and you remove the reason people abandon the deal before it begins.

Stream 02 · FinanceMaking the complex feel simple

Finance is where confidence goes to die. Four acronyms, a fear of being trapped, and numbers nobody explains.

The industry leads with the jargon and lets the buyer drown. We did the opposite — collapsing four finance types into two plain-language choices: pay less now and decide later, or own it outright at the end. Helen guides the rest.

  • A 5-step guided quote written like a conversation, in under two minutes.
  • The mileage explainer — "8,000 miles is like driving an hour a day."
  • Worked examples on demand — the jargon stays available, never compulsory.
4 → 2
Finance types into plain-language choices
<2 min
Design target — registration to quote
0
Mandatory jargon to reach a number
↳ Trust mechanism

Plain language, a named human, worked examples. Confidence in finance isn't won with a better calculator — it's won by removing every moment the buyer feels stupid for not knowing a term.

Stream 03 · The blended journeyStitched into search, anchored to the forecourt

"Online versus forecourt" was the wrong question. Buyers wanted to start the deal from the sofa — and finish it like a human.

The disruptors built destinations and asked people to buy blind. Auto Trader didn't need a destination — it had the audience and the trust. The job was to thread finance, part-exchange, reservations and delivery into the vehicle page itself, while keeping the forecourt as the anchor for the moments that still need a human.

  • Reserve for £199, fully refundable — commitment without risk.
  • Click & collect or home delivery — the bridge that makes distance feel safe.
  • The forecourt stays the trust anchor — inspection and test-drive kept, not designed out.
The realised visionDriveMatch & the Seller PromiseThe reframe shipped as a trust-anchored vehicle page: pricing, finance and part-exchange inline, every confidence signal on one screen, and a badge that makes the guarantees impossible to miss.
£199
Fully-refundable reservation — commit without risk
14 day
Moneyback guarantee on remote purchases
1
Journey — buy, sell, discover, deliver
↳ Trust mechanism

Start online, finish human. Confidence didn't come from removing the dealer — it came from letting people do the anxious admin privately, then arrive already sure.

E How it came together
Method

Lo-fi first, tested early

Every flow ran through task-based usability sessions before it was resolved into UI — measuring comprehension and confidence, not just clicks.

Brand

BX → UX → UI

Decisions ran through the brand-experience wheel and five fundamentals. "Helen" — the calm, jargon-free voice from Finance & GPX — was protected, never diluted.

Built for everyone

17 to 70+

Designed for both segments and every ability: confident enough for the Connoisseur, reassuring enough for the Explorer.

D4D · Phase 1
Deep Customer Empathy

Depth interviews + market evidence. The real job: confidence.

D4D · Phase 2
Go Broad → Go Narrow

In/out-of-sector scan narrowed to one bet: trust as the product.

D4D · Phase 3 ✓
Rapid Experiments

GPX, Finance and the blended journey — prototyped, tested, realised.

Three friction points. One answer at each: make trust something the buyer can feel before they commit.

A part-exchange price stopped being a fight. Finance stopped being a test you could fail. And the forecourt stopped being a wall between browsing and buying. None of it asked the customer to buy blind — it asked them to start the deal with confidence and finish it on their terms. That is what "moving customers to action" actually meant.

⌁ Hindsight

The asset-light, trust-anchored call drew the line exactly where the research said it should. The disruptor that ignored the forecourt — and burned cash to own the whole transaction — later collapsed. The blended bet aged well.

Evidence base · 2019–2021

PRIMARY · Projective depth interviews, 5 filmed online sessions (Capuchin, Jul–Aug 2021) · Auto Trader internal discovery, "Moving customers to action" (Miro / workshop deck) · Retail Branding & Finance competitive benchmark.
SUPPORTING · Auto Trader FY2021 results & consumer research (5,000 respondents) · GForces · Cox Automotive Insight 2020 · Car Dealer Magazine · Cazoo (CNBC / NYSE-AJAX / H1 2021) · KPMG & Capgemini (reluctance counter-evidence) · Edelman Trust Barometer.
